                                                                     New Series

                                       This issue introduces data on the number of nonsupervisory workers,
                                       along with their hours and earnings, at the division level, for transporta-
                                       tion and public utilities, and services. (See tables B-2, C-1, C-2, and C-7.)

                                                                                                             120
                                                                                         SEASONAL FACTORS
                          The
                  which have
                  marks. These factors will be revised at the time the industry < , ,                    _
                  and more current data are available. The seasonal movements are measured in order to adjust the data statistically for
                  such recurring events as warm and cold weather, crop-growing cycles, holidays, vacations, regular industry model change-
                  over periods, and the like. These movements are generally the largest single component of month-to-month changes in
                  employment, hours, and labor turnover. The seasonal factors which follow enable the analyst to remove these influences
                  from the data in order to determine more basic trends.
                          These factors are to be used with data adjusted to the March 1969 benchmark.
                      1: Seasonal adjustment factors for employees on nonagricultural payrolls, by industry division and groups

                                                               Technical Note

                                                    Additional information concerning the preparation
                                                    of the labor force, employment, hours and earnings,
                                                    and labor turnover series—concepts and scope,
                                                    survey methods, and limitations—is contained in
                                                    technical notes for each of these series, available
                                                    from the Bureau of Labor Statistics free of charge.


                 INTRODUCTION                                                        Relation between the household and payroll series

                 three major sources: (1) household interviews, (2) pay-             another, each providing significant types of information
                 roll reports from employers, and (3) administrative                 that the other cannot suitably supply. Population charac-

                                                                Establishment Data
                  COLLECTION                                                         State and area series and then send the establishment

                     Payroll reports provide current information on wage
                 and salary employment, hours, earnings, and labor turn-              Shuttle Schedules
                 over in nonagricultural establishments, by industry and
                 geographic location.                                                    Two types of data collection schedules arje used:
                                                                                     Form BLS 790-Monthly Report on Employment, Payroll,
                  Federal-State Cooperation
                                                                                     and Hours; and Form DL 1219-Monthly Report on Job
                     Under cooperative arrangements with State agencies,             Openings and Labor Turnover. These schedules are of
                 the respondent fills out a single employment or labor               the " s h u t t l e " type, with space for each month of the

                                                          UNEMPLOYMENT INSURANCE DATA
                    Insured unemployment represents the number of per-               vacations. The rate of insured unemployment is the
                 sons reporting a week of unemployment under an unem-                number of insured unemployed expressed as a percent
                 ployment insurance program. It includes some persons                of average covered employment in a 12-month period
                 who are working part time who would be counted as em-               ending 6 to 8 months prior to the week of reference. Initial
                 ployed in the payroll and household surveys. Excluded               claims are notices filed by those losing jobs covered by
                 are persons who have exhausted their benefit rights                 an unemployment insurance program thatthey are starting
                 and workers who have not earned rights to unemployment              a period of unemployment. A claimant who continues to
                 insurance. In general, excluded from coverage are those             be unemployed a full week is then counted in the insured
                 persons who worked in firms whose size excluded them                unemployment figure.

                                                                      ATTENTION
                      As discussed in the Technical Note, the Bureau periodically adjusts the industry employment series to a recent
                 benchmark to improve their accuracy. These adjustments may also affect the hours and earnings series because employ-
                 ment levels are used as weights. Industry data for all national series shown in this report have been adjusted to March
                 1969 benchmarks. Data from April 196^forward are subject to revision at the time of the next benchmark.

                       Beginning with the June 1970 and subsequent issues of Employment and Earnings, the national data in sections B,
                 C, and D supersede those published in previous issues, as well as those appearing in the Handbook of Labor Statistics,
                 1970. Comparable data will be published in Employment and Earnings, United States, 1909-70, BLS Bulletin 1312-7.
